key_insight: PICASA uses cross-attention on multi-patient single-cell RNA-seq to separate shared cell-type programs from patient-specific cancer signals.
clinical_relevance: By isolating residual patient-specific expression patterns, the method may help reveal individualized tumor mechanisms and biomarkers relevant to precision oncology.
method: Trains a cross-attention neural network on single-cell transcriptomic data from multiple patients.
Learns shared cell-type embeddings and cell-type-specific gene-gene interaction patterns.
Extracts residual signals after accounting for common cell-type effects to highlight patient-specific signatures.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports successful decomposition in three cancer datasets—breast, ovarian, and lung—and notes that many recovered patient-specific signatures were consistent with somatic/genetic features such as CNVs.
watch_item: The approach assumes cell types are broadly shared across patients, so performance may be weaker when tumor composition differs substantially or shared cell-type assumptions do not hold.
paper_title: Decomposing patient heterogeneity of single-cell cancer data by cross-attention neural networks
